subject: Mapp v. Ohio (1961) and the exclusionary rule — illegally seized evidence in state courts
central_question: If the police search your home illegally, can what they find there still be used to convict you?
viewer_promise: By the end you'll understand the exclusionary rule — why American courts throw out evidence police obtain through an illegal search — how a 1957 search for a bombing suspect in Cleveland turned Dollree Mapp into the most important name in search-and-seizure law, and why this one rule is the only thing that makes the Fourth Amendment more than 'a form of words.'
surprise: Police forced their way into Mapp's home hunting a bombing suspect and left arresting her for obscene books in a trunk — she was convicted of obscenity, not the search — yet her appeal became the landmark that reshaped police searches in every state. And the rule she won is the most controversial in criminal law: it sometimes frees the guilty 'because the constable has blundered.'
stakes: Whether the Fourth Amendment's ban on unreasonable searches has any real teeth in a state courtroom — and the price society accepts to enforce it: sometimes letting guilty people go in order to keep the police honest.
target_audience: English-speaking US general audience interested in law, history, policing and how systems actually work
series_thread_from_prev: Gideon guaranteed you a lawyer if you can't afford one. Mapp answers the next question that leaves: what happens when the police break the rules to get the evidence against you in the first place?
demand_evidence: 'Illegal search', 'Fourth Amendment' and 'can the police do that' are very high-recognition, high-search topics in US culture.
Search-and-seizure / your-rights-with-police content sustains durable long-tail interest and is highly shareable.
Mapp v. Ohio is a standard landmark in US civics/law curricula — evergreen educational demand.
competition: Most explainers narrate the obscenity-trial drama; few make the exclusionary rule itself — the hidden enforcement mechanism and its cost — the spine of the story.
differentiation: Frames Mapp not as an obscenity case but as the origin of the rule that actually enforces the Fourth Amendment against the states — and confronts the uncomfortable trade-off (the guilty sometimes go free) instead of dodging it. The hidden room behind 'you can't do that without a warrant.'
research_feasibility: High: the opinion is public domain (367 U.S. 643); CourtListener/Oyez/Justia carry the full opinion, syllabus and 1961 oral argument. Overruled precedent Wolf v. Colorado (338 U.S. 25) is also public domain.
visual_feasibility: High: 1950s Cleveland home + forced entry (symbolic), a fake 'warrant' paper, police silhouettes, a trunk of papers, the Fourth Amendment text, SCOTUS exterior/chamber (reuse), scales/handcuffs motifs. All symbolic reconstruction, no real likeness.
risk_notes: Decided landmark (1961); parties deceased/institutional; opinion is public domain — advertiser-friendly.

Obscenity is adjacent to the facts: frame strictly around the SEARCH and the Fourth Amendment; do NOT depict, describe, or dwell on the seized 'obscene' materials (advertiser-safety + good taste). Keep it about police power, not sex.
Dollree Mapp is deceased (2014); describe her arrest/record accurately and neutrally; charged vs convicted distinction.
Generated visuals are symbolic reconstruction, never presented as authentic footage (invariant 11).
kill_conditions: Cannot source the holding (exclusionary rule applied to states via the 14th Amendment, overruling Wolf v. Colorado) to 367 U.S. 643 plus one independent reference.
